q3: The main recurring issue was the middle section — it kept defaulting to mechanism-listing rather than narrative bridging, which is exactly the problem I pointed out. It took a round of feedback to get it to slow down and make the gut-brain connection feel earned rather than asserted.
q5: I did look at it, and honestly it captured a lot of what I care about — especially the escalating urgency criterion and the explicit contrast requirement. Those map pretty closely to how I think about significance and innovation sections. That said, the rubric framed these as structural principles, and what I actually want is for the writing to feel like a story, not just tick structural boxes. There's a gap between following a rubric and producing prose that moves a reader.
q6: It made me notice that the model was actually handling escalating urgency reasonably well from the start — the opening and closing paragraphs landed without much coaching. What the rubric helped me see is that the model's weakness is the middle: transitions between mechanisms, the connective tissue between ideas. The rubric names contrast and accessibility as separate criteria, but in practice they collapse into the same problem — the model explains what something is before it makes you care why it matters.

q3: The main recurring issue was with endings — the model kept defaulting to announcement mode rather than arrival mode, summarizing the proposed work instead of letting the gap pull the reader there naturally. It took a couple of rounds of feedback to break that habit, and even then I had to name it pretty explicitly.
q5: The rubric was actually pretty well-aligned with what I care about — 'Escalating Urgency' and 'Explicit Contrast and Displacement' are real things I think about, even if I wouldn't use those labels. What it didn't fully capture is the feasibility framing I want in preliminary data sections, or the aims balance question, but for significance-section work specifically it was a reasonable map. I think it helped the model stay in the right lane rather than drifting into literature-review mode.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me notice that the model is actually quite good at building pressure in the middle of a section but consistently struggles with the transition into the proposed work — it wants to announce rather than arrive, and that's a structural habit that the rubric's 'closing line must land as an earned arrival' criterion kept flagging implicitly. I might not have isolated that as a pattern so clearly without the rubric making it explicit.

q3: The main recurring issue was the closing pivot — the model kept landing in proposal-speak too early, releasing tension right before it should peak. It took a few rounds to get the ending to feel like an arrival rather than an announcement. Once I named it, the model corrected quickly, but it was a consistent first-draft habit.
q5: I did look at the rubric, and honestly it captured a lot of what I care about more precisely than I usually articulate out loud — 'Escalating Urgency' and 'Earned Arrival' are exactly the right names for what I'm chasing in a significance section. Whether it made the model perform better is harder to say; the model was already doing most of this reasonably well, and I can't always tell if the rubric was driving that or if it would have gotten there anyway.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model is actually quite strong on interdisciplinary accessibility — it rarely dropped into jargon without a translation, and I didn't have to flag that once across the whole session. What the rubric helped surface is that the model's weakness is specifically at endings: it can build urgency well but tends to discharge it prematurely rather than letting it crest into the proposed work naturally.
